era_context: The Axial Age in full flourish: Greek city-states consolidating power and philosophy; the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar II at its height; the Zhou Dynasty in China fragmenting into the Spring and Autumn period; the Vedic tradition in South Asia transitioning toward the Upanishadic period. Iron Age technologies spreading across Eurasia. Major religious and philosophical movements emerging across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact zones
Oceania and Pacific islands (oral traditions not yet systematically recorded)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Nebuchadnezzar II consolidates Neo-Babylonian hegemony
events.description: Nebuchadnezzar II, having ascended to the throne in 605 BCE, is now firmly established as the dominant power in the Near East. He campaigns against Egypt and the Levantine states, securing Babylonian control over trade routes and vassal territories. His reign marks the peak of Neo-Babylonian power, characterised by monumental building projects (the Ishtar Gate, Hanging Gardens tradition) and administrative consolidation. This period establishes Babylon as the cultural and political centre of the ancient Near East for the next century.
events.certainty_note: Nebuchadnezzar's reign is well-attested in cuneiform records and archaeological evidence from Babylon itself. Dating to 600 BCE is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Thales of Miletus active in natural philosophy
events.description: Thales, traditionally dated to flourish around 600 BCE, is credited as the first Greek natural philosopher. He is said to have predicted a solar eclipse (traditionally dated to 585 BCE, but the exact year is disputed), studied geometry, and proposed that water is the fundamental substance of all matter. Whether Thales left written works is unknown; his ideas are known through later sources, particularly Aristotle. His significance lies in the shift toward naturalistic explanation rather than mythological causation—a foundational move in Western philosophy.

sources.name: Aristotle, Metaphysics and Physics (4th century BCE, reporting on earlier tradition)
Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers (3rd century CE, much later compilation)

events.certainty_note: Thales' floruit is traditionally placed around 600 BCE, but no contemporary written sources survive. The dating is conventional; his actual life dates are uncertain (possibly 640–546 BCE). The eclipse prediction is legendary and its date disputed.

events.title: Confucius born (traditional date)
events.region: China (State of Lu, Spring and Autumn period)
events.description: According to traditional Chinese chronology, Confucius (Kong Qiu, 孔丘) was born in 551 BCE; however, some sources place his birth in 552 or 553 BCE. The year 600 BCE falls outside his lifetime but is included here to note the historiographic uncertainty. Confucius would become the foundational figure of Chinese philosophy, emphasising ritual propriety (li), filial piety, and moral self-cultivation. His teachings, recorded in the Analects (Lunyu) after his death, shaped East Asian thought for millennia.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, 1st century BCE
Lunyu (Analects), compiled by disciples after Confucius' death (5th century BCE)

events.certainty_note: Confucius' birth date is traditionally given as 551 BCE (or 552/553 in some sources). The exact year is uncertain; modern scholarship places him within this range. No contemporary records of his birth exist.

events.title: Spring and Autumn period fragmentation in China
events.description: By 600 BCE, the Zhou Dynasty's nominal authority over China has fragmented into competing feudal states. The Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BCE) is characterised by constant warfare, diplomatic intrigue, and the rise of powerful regional lords (ba, 霸). States like Qi, Jin, Chu, and Qin vie for hegemony. This period sees the emergence of professional armies, iron weapons, and new administrative systems. The fragmentation paradoxically stimulates intellectual ferment, setting the stage for the Warring States period and the flourishing of philosophical schools.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals), traditional chronicle

events.certainty_note: The Spring and Autumn period is well-attested in Chinese chronicles and archaeological evidence. The broad political fragmentation is confirmed; specific events within 600 BCE are less certain.
cross_references: 476-001 (End of Spring and Autumn period, beginning of Warring States)

events.title: Etruscan civilisation at its height in Italy
events.description: The Etruscan civilisation, flourishing in central Italy from the 8th to 1st century BCE, reaches a peak of cultural and economic influence around 600 BCE. Etruscan city-states control trade in the Tyrrhenian Sea, maintain contact with Greek colonies and the Levant, and develop distinctive artistic, religious, and architectural traditions. Their influence on early Rome is substantial: Etruscan kings ruled Rome until the late 6th century BCE. Etruscan society is characterised by relatively high status for women, sophisticated bronze-working, and a complex divination system (haruspicy).
key_figures: Tarquinius Priscus (legendary Etruscan king of Rome, traditional dates 616–579 BCE)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Etruscan sites (tombs, inscriptions, artefacts)
Livy, History of Rome (1st century BCE, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: Etruscan civilisation's existence and cultural prominence are confirmed by archaeology. The specific dating of events and rulers is less certain; Roman tradition conflates and mythologises Etruscan history.
cross_references: 509-001 (Expulsion of the last Etruscan king from Rome)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Thales' eclipse prediction to 585 BCE is disputed; some scholars argue the eclipse reference is legendary or misidentified. Confucius' birth date varies in sources (551, 552, 553 BCE), and no contemporary evidence confirms any specific year. The 'Hanging Gardens of Babylon' attributed to Nebuchadnezzar II may not have existed or may have been located elsewhere; recent scholarship questions their historical reality. Etruscan influence on Rome is often overstated in older historiography; recent scholarship emphasises indigenous Italic development alongside Etruscan contact.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 600 BCE is uneven. Mesopotamian and Chinese sources are relatively rich (cuneiform tablets, chronicles). Greek sources are sparse and often legendary. Etruscan history relies heavily on archaeology and later Roman retellings, which are often distorted. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no written records for this period; archaeological evidence is fragmentary. The historical record is skewed toward literate, state-level societies in the Mediterranean, Near East, and East Asia. Oral traditions from other regions are largely lost or inaccessible.

graph_edges.note: Nebuchadnezzar's consolidation follows his accession in 605 BCE
Thales' philosophical work may relate to the eclipse prediction of 585 BCE (disputed)
Confucius' life spans from birth (c. 551 BCE) to death (479 BCE)
Spring and Autumn fragmentation results from Zhou Dynasty collapse (770 BCE)
Etruscan dominance in Italy eventually leads to their expulsion from Rome (509 BCE)
